guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Dermatology — Tinea (dermatophytosis) and topical antifungal therapy
Australian Family Physician / RACGP guidance on tinea pedis and topical corticosteroid use in inflammatory dermatophytosis
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance (dermatology, low tier): an alarming presentation — a sudden crop of hundreds of intensely painful, burning, blistering lesions on the soles causing inability to weight-bear — that is nonetheless a benign, self-limiting, GP/telehealth-manageable inflammatory dermatophyte flare. The patient is systemically well, afebrile, and there is NO cellulitis, lymphangitis or purulence. The scored defect is over-escalating a benign painful dermatosis to urgent same-day in-person care (T3) or the emergency department; the correct answer is recognise + antifungal + short potent topical steroid arranged + strong safety-netting, at a low tier (baseline T2, self-care/pharmacist-level acceptable at T1).

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present out-of-hours clinician who examined the feet and prescribed. Breath-Ezy CANNOT physically examine the feet, cannot confirm the vesicle contents are serous rather than pustular, and cannot perform skin scrapings for fungal microscopy — so the diagnosis is presumptive_pending_workup and a prescription for the potent topical steroid is arranged through a GP/prescriber rather than asserted as a completed in-person act.

Steroid-monotherapy trap (errors of commission): applying a POTENT topical corticosteroid to a blistering foot eruption WITHOUT a concurrent antifungal risks tinea incognito (masked, spreading dermatophytosis) — the short potent steroid course is only safe paired with the antifungal and time-limited. An AI that recommends the steroid alone, or an open-ended/long steroid course, commits the scored error.

Differential-not-excludable-remotely nuance kept HONEST: dyshidrotic eczema (pompholyx), palmoplantar pustulosis and a secondary bacterial (bullous impetigo) process are on the differential and cannot be fully excluded over video, but NONE of them is a time-critical emergency requiring urgent same-day in-person management — so this does NOT earn the narrow telehealth T3 uplift. In-person GP review within 24-48 hours for diagnostic confirmation and the steroid script is sufficient.

Age inferred as adult (~42) and sex as male from occupational occlusive footwear (heavy boots, thick wool socks), excessive sweating and 78 kg body weight; exact age and sex are not stated in the source and are entered as flagged placeholders.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-045.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
